card.indications: Prevention of organ-transplant rejection; treatment of selected autoimmune diseases; and treatment of dry-eye disease with ophthalmic products. Formulations are not automatically interchangeable.
card.mechanism: Binds cyclophilin and inhibits calcineurin, reducing T-cell activation and interleukin-2 production.
card.dosing: Dose is individualized by indication, formulation, body weight, trough concentration, renal function, and adverse effects. Modified and nonmodified oral products are not interchangeable without specialist guidance.
card.routeAdministration: Give at the same times and consistently with regard to meals. Measure oral solution accurately and use the specified diluent. IV administration requires close monitoring for anaphylaxis. Ophthalmic products are used only in the eyes.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to cyclosporine or formulation ingredients. Additional contraindications vary by indication, including uncontrolled hypertension, malignancy, or abnormal renal function in some nontransplant uses.
card.warnings: Can cause nephrotoxicity, hypertension, serious infection, malignancy, hepatotoxicity, neurotoxicity, hyperkalemia, and thrombotic microangiopathy. Numerous interactions can markedly alter drug levels.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Tremor, headache, hypertension, kidney dysfunction, hypertrichosis, gingival overgrowth, nausea, diarrhea, and increased lipids.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Renal failure, severe hypertension, seizures, encephalopathy, serious infection, lymphoma or skin cancer, hepatotoxicity, anaphylaxis, and thrombotic microangiopathy.
card.interactions: CYP3A4 and P-gp inhibitors increase concentrations; inducers decrease them. Nephrotoxic drugs, potassium-raising drugs, statins, colchicine, and grapefruit can cause serious toxicity.
card.monitoring: Monitor trough levels when ordered, serum creatinine, BUN, blood pressure, potassium, magnesium, liver enzymes, lipids, CBC, infection, neurologic status, and oral health.
card.patientTeaching: Use the exact brand and formulation prescribed. Do not change products independently. Report decreased urine, swelling, severe headache, tremor, fever, mouth sores, or unusual skin lesions. Use sun protection.
card.nutrition: Keep meal timing consistent. Avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice. Follow potassium and sodium guidance and maintain good oral hygiene.

dosageForms: Oral pellets in selected pediatric products

card.indications: Reduction of stroke and systemic embolism risk in nonvalvular atrial fibrillation; treatment and prevention of venous thromboembolism depending on age and product.
card.mechanism: Directly and reversibly inhibits thrombin, reducing conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin and thrombus formation.
card.dosing: Dose depends on indication, renal function, age, and interacting drugs. Renal impairment may require dose reduction or avoidance.
card.routeAdministration: Swallow capsules whole with a full glass of water. Do not crush, chew, or open capsules because exposure can increase. Keep capsules in the original container and protect from moisture.
card.contraindications: Active pathologic bleeding, serious hypersensitivity, and mechanical prosthetic heart valves.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: premature discontinuation increases thrombotic risk; spinal or epidural hematoma may cause paralysis. Major or fatal bleeding can occur. Idarucizumab is the specific reversal agent for urgent situations.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dyspepsia, abdominal discomfort, gastritis-like symptoms, nausea, and minor bleeding.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Major gastrointestinal or intracranial bleeding, spinal hematoma, anaphylaxis, thrombocytopenia, and thromboembolism after interruption.
card.interactions: P-gp inhibitors or inducers can alter exposure, especially with renal impairment. NSAIDs, antiplatelet drugs, and other anticoagulants increase bleeding.
card.monitoring: Monitor renal function, CBC, signs of bleeding, adherence, neurologic symptoms after neuraxial procedures, and timing around surgery. Routine INR monitoring is not used.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly on schedule and do not stop without prescriber approval. Report black stools, vomiting blood, severe headache, weakness, or unusual bleeding. Tell all clinicians and dentists.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food; food may reduce stomach upset. Avoid excessive alcohol and unapproved NSAIDs or herbal products that increase bleeding.

card.indications: Treatment of chronic spasticity from upper motor-neuron disorders and emergency treatment or prevention of malignant hyperthermia.
card.mechanism: Blocks calcium release from skeletal-muscle sarcoplasmic reticulum by inhibiting ryanodine receptors, reducing excitation-contraction coupling.
card.dosing: Oral therapy begins low and is titrated gradually. Malignant-hyperthermia treatment uses rapid weight-based IV dosing and repeat doses until symptoms are controlled.
card.routeAdministration: Oral doses may be taken with food. IV product requires exact reconstitution and rapid administration during malignant hyperthermia. Monitor respiratory and muscle function closely.
card.contraindications: Active liver disease or significant hepatic dysfunction for oral therapy; spasticity needed to maintain posture, balance, or function; hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for potentially fatal hepatotoxicity with oral use. Can cause profound weakness, respiratory depression, dysphagia, aspiration, and photosensitivity.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Weakness, drowsiness, dizziness, fatigue, diarrhea, nausea, and abdominal discomfort.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Fatal hepatitis, respiratory failure, severe muscle weakness, aspiration pneumonia, pleural or pericardial effusion, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: CNS depressants increase sedation. Verapamil or diltiazem with IV dantrolene may cause hyperkalemia and cardiovascular collapse. Other hepatotoxic drugs increase liver risk.
card.monitoring: Monitor liver function before and during oral therapy, muscle strength, respiratory status, swallowing, bowel pattern, and response of spasticity. During malignant hyperthermia monitor temperature, potassium, acid-base status, rhythm, urine output, and CK.
card.patientTeaching: Report jaundice, dark urine, severe fatigue, abdominal pain, breathing difficulty, or worsening weakness. Avoid alcohol and excessive sun. Do not drive until effects are known.
card.nutrition: Take with food if stomach upset occurs. Avoid alcohol. Maintain hydration during treatment of malignant hyperthermia and follow protein or calorie guidance if weakness affects intake.

card.indications: Treatment of anemia due to chronic kidney disease and anemia from myelosuppressive chemotherapy in selected patients.
card.mechanism: Stimulates erythroid progenitor cells in bone marrow to increase red blood cell production.
card.dosing: Dose is individualized by indication, hemoglobin, rate of rise, iron status, and transfusion needs. Use the lowest dose sufficient to reduce transfusions.
card.routeAdministration: Do not shake. Inspect for particles or discoloration. Give IV or subcutaneously as ordered. Rotate subcutaneous sites. Verify adequate iron stores before and during therapy.
card.contraindications: Uncontrolled hypertension, pure red-cell aplasia after an erythropoietin product, or serious hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include increased risk of death, myocardial infarction, stroke, venous thromboembolism, tumor progression or recurrence, and vascular-access thrombosis when hemoglobin is targeted too high.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Hypertension, edema, headache, injection-site discomfort, diarrhea, fever, and dyspnea.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Stroke, myocardial infarction, thrombosis, hypertensive crisis, seizures, pure red-cell aplasia, severe skin reactions, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: No major metabolic interactions, but response is reduced by iron, folate, or vitamin B12 deficiency, inflammation, blood loss, or inadequate dialysis.
card.monitoring: Monitor hemoglobin frequently during initiation and dose changes, blood pressure, iron studies, CBC, transfusion needs, thrombosis, seizures, and tumor response in oncology patients.
card.patientTeaching: Keep laboratory appointments and monitor blood pressure if instructed. Report chest pain, sudden weakness, severe headache, leg swelling, shortness of breath, seizures, rash, or loss of treatment effect.
card.nutrition: Ensure adequate iron, folate, vitamin B12, protein, and calories as ordered. Oral or IV iron supplementation is often required.

card.indications: Treatment of chronic iron overload from repeated blood transfusions and selected non-transfusion-dependent thalassemia syndromes.
card.mechanism: Binds ferric iron with high affinity and promotes excretion mainly through feces.
card.dosing: Dose is based on body weight, iron burden, transfusion history, formulation, renal and hepatic function, and response. Different formulations are not milligram-for-milligram interchangeable.
card.routeAdministration: Follow formulation-specific directions. Dispersible tablets are suspended in liquid and not swallowed whole. Film-coated tablets or granules may be taken on an empty stomach or with a light meal as labeled.
card.contraindications: Severe renal impairment, poor performance status, high-risk myelodysplastic syndrome or advanced malignancy in some labels, platelet count below the labeled threshold, or hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings for renal failure, hepatic failure, and gastrointestinal hemorrhage, which may be fatal. Also may cause cytopenias, severe skin reactions, hearing loss, and ocular toxicity.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, rash, headache, and increased serum creatinine.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Acute kidney injury, Fanconi syndrome, hepatic failure, GI ulceration or hemorrhage, agranulocytosis, thrombocytopenia, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, hearing loss, and vision changes.
card.interactions: Avoid aluminum-containing antacids. Nephrotoxic or hepatotoxic drugs and anticoagulants or NSAIDs increase risk. May alter concentrations of drugs metabolized by CYP3A4, CYP2C8, or CYP1A2.
card.monitoring: Monitor serum creatinine, urinalysis, liver tests, CBC, ferritin, iron burden, hearing, vision, weight, hydration, and GI bleeding. Monitoring is especially frequent during initiation.
card.patientTeaching: Use only the prescribed formulation and preparation method. Report decreased urine, dark stools, vomiting blood, jaundice, severe rash, fever, hearing changes, or vision changes.
card.nutrition: Take according to formulation-specific meal instructions. Maintain hydration, especially during vomiting or diarrhea. Avoid aluminum-containing antacids.
